data.body:
=== RESEARCH REPORT ===
=== research: scoped-vs-broad + convergence + why-peers-avoid
=== agent a73f452589b798e45

All five load-bearing claims confirmed against primary sources. I now have verified evidence across all five research angles plus adversarial verification. Let me synthesize the final report.

---

# LLM Review of Implementation Plans: The Convergence Tension

Research report. All load-bearing claims verified against primary sources; thin/contested evidence flagged inline.

## Executive answer

The tension you named is real and well-documented, and the field has converged on a clear resolution: **scoped, criterion-anchored checks for the part that must gate (convergent), with broad open-ended LLM feedback kept advisory and bounded (non-blocking, capped iterations).** Nobody runs unbounded open-ended LLM review as a hard gate, and the reasons are evidence-backed, not merely caution. When the *executor* is an LLM rather than a human, the value of up-front broad review goes **up** (the largest single class of agent failure is upstream specification/plan defects, and an LLM executor won't push back on a bad plan the way a human would) — but that does not change the convergence mechanics, so the broad pass still needs a hard bound.

---

## 1. How the field handles scoped-vs-broad: hybrid, with a structured spine

No mature system is purely one or the other. The dominant shape is a **hybrid**: a fixed, structured "rubric" spine that is stable and comparable, with open-ended free-text findings riding on top.

- **PR-Agent / Qodo Merge** (OSS, The-PR-Agent/pr-agent, ~11.7k stars, actively maintained) is the clearest fixed-rubric example: `/review` emits named structured fields — `review effort [x/5]`, `possible security issue`, `ticket compliance` (4-valued) — plus a severity/category findings table. The rubric is the stable skeleton; prose findings hang off it.
- **CodeRabbit** (commercial) runs a holistic pass but anchors it with a **fixed set of 4 built-in checks** (docstring coverage, PR title, description, scope) plus opt-in custom checks.
- **Greptile** leans holistic over a repo-wide graph but bolts on **confidence scores per comment** and a **severity threshold** as the primary volume knob.
- **Research mirrors this.** Decomposed, rubric/checklist-grounded evaluation reliably beats a single holistic score on agreement, consistency, and bias: RLCF / "Checklists Are Better Than Reward Models" (Apple+CMU, NeurIPS 2025, arXiv 2507.18624; +4 FollowBench / +6 InFoBench / +3 Arena-Hard — verified), Prometheus (ICLR 2024; Pearson 0.897 with humans vs GPT-4's 0.882), FLASK (ICLR 2024, 12 fine-grained skills), Branch-Solve-Merge (Meta/UNC; up to +26% human agreement, position bias cut up to 50%).
- **The honest counter:** "Who Validates the Validators?" (UIST 2024) shows criteria are *discovered by grading outputs* ("criteria drift") — i.e., the rubric isn't free; open-ended review is what surfaces the right criteria in the first place. And one contested single-author preprint ("Rethinking Atomic Decomposition," 2026, arXiv 2603.28005) argues decomposition's gains may partly be a "more reasoning tokens" artifact rather than decomposition per se. **Flag: contested.** Net: the two modes are complementary — open-ended review *finds* criteria; the checklist makes scoring them convergent.

The plan-approval analog (closest to your use case) lives in the **agent tools**, not the PR reviewers: **Devin's Planning Checkpoint**, **OpenHands Planning Mode** (~78k stars), **Claude Code plan mode / ExitPlanMode**, **GitHub Copilot Workspace** (task→spec→plan→code, two human edit points), **GitHub Spec Kit** (Specify→Plan→Tasks→Implement with a Plan Review gate), and **AWS Kiro** (requirements→design→tasks, "you approve each one before the next begins"). In every one of these, **the plan gate is a human approval**, and the LLM's role is to draft/critique, not to block.

## 2. Convergence techniques, ranked by strength of evidence

How to make a revise→re-review loop actually terminate. Ranked best-to-worst by evidentiary support:

1. **Fixed iteration cap (strongest, near-universal).** Self-Refine caps at 4 and reports explicit diminishing returns (code-opt gains +5.0 / +0.9 / +0.7 across iterations; most tasks plateau by ~3). Constitutional AI caps at 4 critique-revision pairs and notes "the first revision does most of the work." MetaGPT caps its QA loop at 3 retries. Anthropic's "Building Effective Agents" explicitly recommends "a maximum number of iterations." This is the most robust and most widely shipped stop condition. **Recommend: cap broad review at ~2-3 passes.**

2. **Severity/confidence gating — block only on enumerated criteria, treat open-ended as advisory (strong, this is the actual industry answer to your tension).** Greptile attaches confidence to every comment and gates on a severity threshold. Cloudflare's production AI review has an *explicit bias toward approval* ("a single warning in an otherwise clean MR still gets `approved_with_comments` rather than a block"). This is precisely "the scoped rubric is the gate; broad findings are advice." Strongly evidenced and the de-facto standard.

3. **External verifier / ground-truth stop signal (strong — but only where a verifiable signal exists).** Reflexion's loop terminates on a real success signal (unit tests, env reward, exact-match) OR a max-trial budget — and it's the *only* critique family showing reliable monotonic gains, precisely because it has ground truth. Hard automated gates in the field are reserved for **deterministic** signals (tests, RLVR, linters/Danger/reviewdog), never LLM judgment. For plan review there usually is no ground truth, which is why this technique is unavailable and you fall back to caps + severity gating.

4. **Diff-from-previous-review / incremental review (strong for noise control).** CodeRabbit's named distinction — incremental `@coderabbitai review` (only new changes since last full review, carrying forward prior comments) vs `full review` (re-litigate everything) — is the single biggest anti-"drowning-in-findings" mechanism in production tooling. It directly attacks non-convergence by not re-opening settled findings. Copilot's reviewer is the cautionary counterexample: it doesn't auto re-review and "may repeat the same comments even if dismissed."

5. **"No new criteria after round 1" / freeze-the-rubric (moderate, indirect support).** Not named as such in the literature, but it is the logical extension of (1)+(2): if the blocking rubric is frozen up front, the gate is convergent by construction and new open-ended findings can only ever be advisory. Constitutional AI's fixed 16-principle constitution is the closest real instance.

6. **Embedding-based dedup / clustering of findings (moderate, single-vendor).** Greptile clusters comments via vector embeddings and filters on user feedback (vendor-reported 19%→55% addressed rate). Promising but vendor-reported. **Flag: thin.**

7. **Human adjudication of the open-ended tail (strong as a fallback, weak as "convergence").** Cloudflare's "break glass" force-approve, "won't fix" overriding the AI, LangGraph's blocking `interrupt()` with Approve/Edit/Reject/Respond. This doesn't make the loop converge — it terminates it by fiat. It's the correct escape hatch, not an algorithm.

**What does NOT work:** relying on the critic to declare "no more issues." No credible system uses "the critic stops finding things" as a stop condition, because intrinsic self-critique is unreliable in both directions. Self-Refine reports ChatGPT says "everything looks good" on 94% of math instances (false-pass). And Huang et al., "LLMs Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet" (ICLR 2024, verified) shows intrinsic self-correction *degrades* accuracy: GSM8K 75.9→75.1→74.7%, CommonSenseQA 75.8→38.1% after one round. The realistic failure of an unbounded loop is therefore **not** infinite productive findings — it is either **premature convergence** ("degeneration-of-thought," Liang et al. 2023: a model locks onto its first stance) or **net-negative over-correction** (a 2026 preprint reports ~22.7% overshoot; **flag: very recent, unverified**).

## 3. Why peers avoid (a) per-dimension reviewers and (b) blocking LLM gates

**(a) Why most ship a single holistic pass rather than many per-dimension LLM reviewers — dominant reason: cost/latency, NOT a quality argument.** The *research* actually favors decomposition (RLCF, FLASK, BSM, Prometheus all show fine-grained criteria improve reliability). So the reason production tools don't fan out into N reviewers is economic: each dimension is another LLM call (latency + token cost on every PR/plan), and the contested "Rethinking Atomic Decomposition" result raises the worry that you may be paying for extra generation rather than genuine accuracy. The compromise everyone reaches is the **structured single pass** (one call, fixed rubric fields) — capturing most of the decomposition benefit at one call's cost. Trend note: Qodo and Greptile v3 are now moving *toward* multi-perspective agents, suggesting cost was the binding constraint and it's loosening.

**(b) Why LLM plan review is advisory, not a hard blocking gate — dominant reason: the false-negative/rubber-stamp problem, reinforced by non-determinism.** Ranking the candidate causes by evidence:

- **Rubber-stamp / false-negative (DOMINANT).** LLM judges measurably accept bad inputs. Self-preference bias is real and largest for GPT-4 (Panickssery et al., NeurIPS 2024; Wataoka et al. 2024). Intrinsic self-correction net-degrades (Huang et al.). "Uncovering Systematic Failures of LLMs in Verifying Code Against NL Specs" (2025) finds even GPT-4-turbo "frequently misclassifies wrong code as correct," which it cites as the direct reason such tools "operate in advisory mode requiring human approval rather than as blocking gates." Cloudflare's rubric is *deliberately* approval-biased. A gate that rubber-stamps is worse than no gate (false security), so nobody trusts it to block.
- **Non-determinism (STRONG SECONDARY).** ~35% of GPT-4 pairwise verdicts flip on answer-order swap alone (Zheng et al., verified); judges are non-deterministic even at temp 0 (model-version drift, FP non-associativity, MoE batch effects). A probabilistic required check is a *flaky* gate — which is exactly why deterministic tools (Danger ~5.5k stars, reviewdog ~9.4k stars) own the hard-block role and LLM bots stay advisory.
- **Cost/latency, immaturity (CONTRIBUTING, not dominant).** Real but secondary; Anthropic/OpenAI guidance frames gating as risk-proportional and explicitly expects it to relax "as confidence in the agent's reliability grows" — i.e., immaturity is treated as temporary, the reliability problem as structural.

So: holistic-vs-decomposed is decided by **cost**; advisory-vs-blocking is decided by **the rubber-stamp/false-negative problem** (with non-determinism as the close-second reason a probabilistic gate is unacceptable).

## 4. Does an LLM executor change the answer? Yes — broad review becomes MORE valuable

The evidence says up-front broad review pays off *more* when an LLM (not a human) implements the plan:

- **Upstream plan/spec defects are the single largest failure class.** MAST, "Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?" (UC Berkeley, arXiv 2503.13657, verified): Specification & System Design ≈ **44.2%** of failures — the largest of three categories. The thing broad plan review targets is the thing that fails most.
- **Early errors compound over long autonomous trajectories.** METR (arXiv 2503.14499, verified): ~100% success on <4-minute tasks, <10% on >4-hour tasks. Per-step error multiplies, not averages ("Solving a Million-Step LLM Task," 2025: a 1% per-step rate guarantees failure within hundreds of steps). A human catches a bad plan mid-stream; the compounding math says an autonomous LLM often cannot.
- **The LLM executor won't push back.** Agents exhibit conformity/sycophancy — "when one agent makes a confident assertion, others tend to align rather than push back," and LLM plans "may lack feasibility without considering adequate preconditions." The human safeguard "this plan is obviously wrong, I'll stop" is largely absent. **Flag: partly vendor-blog framing (Galileo), but corroborated by the planning survey arXiv 2402.02716.**
- **Counter-consideration:** an LLM executor can re-plan cheaply mid-execution, partially substituting for perfect up-front review — but re-planning "frequently causes off-task behavior" (web-agent study, arXiv 2603.14248), so it's an unreliable safety net that *strengthens* the case for up-front review rather than replacing it.

Caveat: this argues broad review is more *valuable*, not that it should *block*. The convergence and rubber-stamp problems are unchanged. So the executor-is-an-LLM case raises the payoff of the **advisory broad pass**, not the gate.

## 5. Recommendation: a scoped convergent core + a bounded broad pass that terminates

A concrete design that resolves the tension:

**Layer 1 — Scoped convergent gate (blocking).** A frozen, enumerated rubric of binary MET/UNMET criteria (e.g., "has acceptance criteria," "names target files," "states verification commands," "no obvious infeasible precondition"). This is your hard gate. Make it convergent by construction:
- Freeze the rubric before round 1; no new *blocking* criteria are added mid-loop.
- Use **binary** criteria, not graded scores (the ternary→binary shift adds ~20pp agreement; **flag: single-source figure**).
- Anchor each verdict with required-reasoning + a citation to the failing criterion (improves inter-judge consistency).
- Where a deterministic check is possible (does the plan reference files that exist? do named commands parse?), use a real tool, not the LLM — that part is genuinely convergent.

**Layer 2 — Bounded broad pass (advisory, non-blocking).** One open-ended frontier-LLM critique of the whole plan, for unknown-unknowns. Make it terminate:
- **Hard cap at 1-2 passes** (Self-Refine/CAI evidence: first pass captures most value; more passes risk net-negative drift).
- **Diff-only re-review:** on a revised plan, evaluate only what changed; carry prior findings forward; never re-open settled ones (CodeRabbit's incremental model).
- Findings are **advisory** — they inform the human/author but cannot block. Optionally let a high-severity broad finding *promote* a proposal to add a Layer-1 criterion **for the next plan**, not this one (freeze-the-rubric, deferred).
- **Human adjudicates the tail:** anything the broad pass surfaces that isn't in the rubric goes to the human approver (LangGraph `interrupt()` / break-glass). This is the only honest terminator for the open-ended part.

**Why this terminates:** the loop's *blocking* condition (Layer 1) is a frozen binary rubric — fixed point reached when all criteria are MET, guaranteed convergent. The *advisory* part (Layer 2) is bounded by an iteration cap and diff-scoping, so it cannot run forever, and because it's non-blocking it cannot prevent the plan from passing. You get the unknown-unknown coverage of broad review without inheriting its non-convergence into the gate.

**Two caveats to flag in your own design:**
1. Don't let the LLM gate its own plan if the same model wrote it — self-preference is highest exactly there (intrinsic self-correction degrades; GPT-4 most self-preferring). Use a different model, or a deterministic check, for the blocking layer.
2. The "binary +20pp agreement" and the "~22.7% overshoot" figures are **single-source / very-recent preprints** — directional, not settled. The load-bearing, peer-reviewed spine is: Huang et al. (self-correction degrades), Zheng et al. (position-flip ~35%), MAST (44% spec failures), METR (long-task collapse), RLCF/Prometheus/FLASK/BSM (rubrics improve convergence), Self-Refine/CAI/MetaGPT/Reflexion (caps + diminishing returns).
